summary: My name is Kenji Lee, and I'm a jazz saxophonist. I was born in Tokyo and raised in Southern California before moving to Ann Arbor to study Jazz at the University of Michigan. After that, I lived and worked in Detroit's vibrant jazz scene, and in 2019 I relocated to Seattle to build a new career there. The coronavirus pandemic has been a major setback for me, especially as I was still establishing myself in Seattle's smaller jazz community. Despite the challenges, I remain optimistic and am considering moving back to Detroit where I have a stronger network.

I was born in Tokyo, Japan, which is where my story begins before moving to the United States. Although I didn't spend much of my career there, Tokyo is important as my place of birth. My musical journey really took off after I left Tokyo and was raised in Southern California. It’s a part of my identity and background that I carry with me.

I was raised in Southern California after being born in Tokyo. Growing up there shaped my early experiences before I moved to Michigan for college. Southern California was where I developed my initial connection to music before pursuing formal jazz studies. It’s a significant part of my upbringing and musical foundation.

I moved to Ann Arbor, Michigan to attend the University of Michigan and pursue a Jazz Studies degree. Ann Arbor was where I transitioned from being a student to a professional musician. It was an important place for my academic and musical development before I moved on to Detroit. The time I spent there laid the groundwork for my career as a jazz saxophonist.

I attended the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor to study Jazz Studies. It was there that I honed my skills and prepared myself for a career as a professional musician. The education and connections I made at the University of Michigan were crucial for my development. After graduating, I moved to Detroit to start working in the jazz scene full-time.

Detroit was where I really established myself as a freelance jazz musician. The music economy there was vibrant and healthy, with many jazz clubs and venues where I played regularly, sometimes three or four nights a week. I also taught music and did recording work, which helped me build multiple income streams. After moving to Seattle, I realized how much stronger the jazz infrastructure in Detroit was, which is why I plan to move back there after the pandemic.

I moved to Seattle in September 2019 to start fresh and build my career in a new jazz scene. Unfortunately, the jazz infrastructure there was much smaller compared to Detroit, with Tula's Jazz Club being the last major venue before it closed shortly after I arrived. I worked hard to build connections and find gigs, but the coronavirus pandemic hit soon after, shutting down live music and making it very difficult to sustain myself. Despite these challenges, I played with notable musicians and taught music, but ultimately I’m considering moving back to Detroit where I have a stronger network.

Tula's Jazz Club was the last real jazz club in Seattle when I arrived in September 2019. Unfortunately, it closed on September 30, just 30 days after I moved there, which was a huge blow to the local jazz community and to me personally. Its closure meant that the already limited opportunities for live jazz performances in Seattle became even scarcer. This loss contributed to the challenges I faced trying to establish myself in the Seattle jazz scene before the pandemic hit.

The coronavirus pandemic has been a major disruption to my career as a freelance musician. It caused all my gigs and live music opportunities to vanish almost overnight, just as I was trying to build my career in Seattle. I lost not only performance income but also side jobs in the restaurant industry. Thankfully, government stimulus and unemployment benefits helped me stay afloat, but the pandemic forced me to rethink my career aspirations and consider more stable options like teaching or music administration.

Though I didn't experience the 2008 financial crisis directly as a musician, I know older musicians who have talked about how that event affected the music scene. They shared stories about how gigs and income disappeared during that time, similar to what happened with the coronavirus pandemic. Hearing their experiences gave me perspective on the instability of a freelance music career and the importance of having a safety net.

I've heard from musicians who lived through the September 11 attacks about how that event disrupted the music scene and their livelihoods. It was a cataclysmic event that showed how quickly everything could change and how fragile a freelance musician's career can be. Their stories helped me understand the challenges of sustaining a music career through major crises like the coronavirus pandemic.

My musical journey has taken place entirely within the United States, from Southern California to Michigan and then to Seattle. Each region has its own unique music scene and opportunities, which have shaped my career as a jazz saxophonist. The U.S. has been both a place of opportunity and challenge, especially during events like the coronavirus pandemic that have affected musicians nationwide. Navigating these different cities and their music economies has been a central part of my story.
